headline: Sam’s onboarding friction shows up as unclear approval/quality bars, which makes him hesitate and ask for help later.
summary_bullets: Sam can do the work but second-guesses fit with team expectations, especially when design and API details don’t align.
His biggest stall is uncertainty about “who should approve what before I push,” plus fuzzy quality bars between quick fixes and core flows.
He waits to ask for feedback because he wants to solve things himself first, and he moves much quicker when feedback is specific and fast.
understanding_paragraph: Sam’s main blocker isn’t task ability; it’s knowing the exact approval steps and quality bar for the kind of change he’s making. When feedback arrives specific and fast, he accelerates, but he currently waits too long and gets stuck silently. The clearest next leverage point is tightening “done” and approval expectations for his next two tickets, then pairing to make the bar visible in practice.

axes.meaning: Weak signal: mild strain from second-guessing, worth watching as onboarding friction persists.
No strong engagement signal; he responds to support and feedback speed, not to time alone.
Defining issue: unclear expectations for approval and quality bars, creating late-stage hesitation.
Worth acting on: he improves with clearer “good looks like” definitions and earlier, specific feedback.

brutal_truth_employee: Sam says, “I can do the tasks, but I still second-guess whether my approach matches team expectations.” He also admits, “I usually wait too long before asking because I want to solve things myself first,” and that “Pairing once or twice a week would probably prevent me from getting stuck silently.”
brutal_truth_manager: The conversation surfaced a very actionable signal—“I am not always sure who should approve what before I push”—and Sam tied speed to “specific and fast” feedback. Next time, go one step further by turning that into a concrete approval map for his next ticket before the meeting ends, rather than stopping at the pairing suggestion.

next_actions.action: Loop in the relevant reviewer(s) and confirm the exact approval sequence for Sam’s next ticket (who approves design, who approves API changes, and when).
Schedule 1–2 pairing sessions on Sam’s next ticket and explicitly walk through the approval bar as they go (what “done” means at each step).
Re-ask with a concrete prompt: for his next two tickets, define “good looks like” and name the specific moment he should ask for feedback—before he waits too long.

watch_for: Within two weeks: if Sam still waits to ask until late, treat it as the same approval/quality ambiguity, not as a personal habit.
Before next 1:1: if pairing doesn’t reduce hesitation, confirm whether the mismatch is still design/API expectations or the reviewer/approval ownership.
